# Chapter 02

[9k2h7mqa] Flynn found the Compound the way you found anything illegal now: by driving past it twice, pretending you were lost, and waiting for the part of the desert that didn’t want you to keep going.

[bq8t1v3n] The map in his head insisted there was nothing here but scrub and a service road that died in sand. His implant agreed, helpfully offering him a route back to the nearest “SafeStop” plaza where the shade was sponsored and the water tasted faintly of plastic.

[m4r7x9cs] He killed the overlay with a blink and kept the wheel steady, hands at ten and two like the lessons had drilled into him even though the pod had done the driving back then. His car was not a pod. It was a dented, legal hybrid with a manual mode no one used and a cabin that smelled like old coffee and anxious sweat.

[0p6d3kfw] The service road narrowed into ruts. Wind had laid sand in ribbons across it. Ahead, a line of dead billboards leaned toward each other like drunks sharing a secret. One still held a faded face—smiling, perfect teeth—promising **LIFE EXTENSION PLANS** in exchange for a monthly fee.

[7h5q1zvt] Flynn passed the billboards and felt the air change. Not cooler, not warmer—just less watched. The little prickling at the base of his skull eased, the one he’d lived with since the first time his implant warned him he was “approaching an unsafe zone.”

[3c9n0xjd] The gate was a trick: two sagging sheets of corrugated metal wired to posts and painted the same color as dust. If you didn’t know it was a gate, you’d think the wind had arranged a pile of junk into a suggestion.

[ut1g8h0r] Flynn stopped twenty yards back and waited. He didn’t honk. Honking was an invitation for attention. He let the engine idle and watched the heat shimmer.

[5n2q6r1b] A shape rose from the shade of a ruined trailer like a dog deciding whether to bite. Man, maybe. Woman. Hard to tell at first: a lean figure with a scarf pulled high, a rifle held low, not pointed but not absent.

[8y6f3p2k] The figure walked closer. A woman, Flynn realized, and not much older than him. Oil on her forearms. Hair tied back in a knot that looked like it had been done with impatience. She took in his plates, his tires, the clean glass of his windshield.

[q1d7m4hx] “You lost,” she said.

[s6k9v0me] “No,” Flynn said, and hated how his voice lifted at the end like a question.

[j3w0x8ta] The woman’s eyes flicked, not to his face, but to the air just above his left temple where the implant’s tiny status light would pulse if it felt chatty. “You bring a leash with you?”

[2z7k5n9p] “It’s muted,” Flynn said.

[k0m3f1ru] “Muting’s not cutting.” She stepped closer, close enough that he could smell gasoline trapped in fabric. “Kill it. Or go back.”

[9s2j4v8c] Flynn swallowed. He had rehearsed this part. He reached up behind his ear and pressed the soft spot where the emergency override lived. His vision flashed white. For a moment there was nothing—no temperature reading, no hydration prompt, no gentle suggestion that he should breathe.

[h7b1q5ld] The silence in his skull was so sudden it felt like going deaf.

[4x8c2m0v] The woman nodded once, as if he’d passed a test. She hooked two fingers under the corrugated sheet and pulled. The gate scraped open with a sound like a throat clearing.

[6j0n7r2s] “Park by the pump,” she said. “Engine off. Hands where I can see them.”

[1m9f6t2q] Flynn drove through and the Compound unfolded slowly, not like a fortress but like a camp that had decided to stay.

[3v2k8p0h] There were trailers—some intact, some missing walls—arranged in a loose circle. In the center, a concrete pad held the bones of an old gas station: a roof with one corner collapsed, two dead pumps, and a sign frame with no sign.

[0c7w5z8n] Under the roof, light bulbs hung on cords like fruit. A generator somewhere thudded a steady heartbeat. The air carried a mix of smells: hot metal, oil, char, and something sweet—mesquite smoke.

[l5y1p3du] Cars sat everywhere. Not pods, not smooth egg-shaped compliance machines, but actual cars—half restored, half disassembled, some perched on blocks, some with their hoods open like mouths.

[t8q0m2vs] He’d seen pictures online, of course. Grainy clips passed around like contraband, showing a V8 roaring for five seconds before the feed cut. He’d watched those clips the way other people watched sunsets. But pictures didn’t have the weight of this place, the way the machines looked like they had survived a war.

[6w4r9h1a] A man stood over an engine bay at the far edge of the circle, shoulders wide, back bent. He was working like the sun was a deadline. Sparks jumped from his hands in bright, brief insects.

[7g2p8c4n] Another person sat on the roof of a trailer, legs dangling, watching the horizon through binoculars. A lookout. Flynn felt his mouth go dry.

[2h6x0n3q] The woman with the rifle—Diesel, he guessed, because the message he’d gotten had signed off *D*—walked him to the dead pump. “Keys,” she said.

[0u3m8k5j] Flynn handed them over. It felt like surrendering a small part of himself.

[n9d2s7fa] Diesel pocketed the keys and motioned him toward the circle. “Cage wants to meet you,” she said, and the way she said the name made it clear Cage was gravity here.

[5a7k2v0m] Flynn followed, trying not to stare like a tourist. Every surface had been patched with something else: sheet metal riveted to wood, tarps weighted with tires, solar panels propped at odd angles. The Compound was ugly in the honest way survival was ugly.

[r2m8y0kc] The fire was already going, though the sun hadn’t fully dropped. It was built low in a steel drum cut in half. Around it, people sat on crate chairs and upturned buckets, hands wrapped around enamel mugs.

[3d1p6h9x] They looked up when he approached. Not hostile, not welcoming. Appraising. A pack measuring a new dog.

[7q0f4n2b] Cage sat with his boots close to the heat, elbows on his knees, the light turning his cheekbones into sharp edges. He was lean, sun-browned, with hair that had once been cut carefully and had grown out into something that made him look perpetually in motion.

[9f3k6m1r] He didn’t stand. He didn’t need to. His attention hit Flynn like a hand on the chest.

[1x6h9q0s] “You’re the kid,” Cage said.

[8v2m0d7p] Flynn hated being called kid. He was twenty-two. That counted for something in the city, where the pods did the driving and the risks were mostly digital. Here it counted for nothing. “Flynn,” he said.

[t0k7p3n5] Cage smiled. It was not unkind, but it was sharp. “Flynn. Like the pilot. You fly?”

[w5m1h8c3] “No,” Flynn said. “I—” He almost said *I can fix things.* He almost said *I’m not useless.* He stopped himself.

[4k3p9v1z] Cage’s eyes flicked to Diesel. “He kill his implant?”

[6p7n2x0d] Diesel nodded. “Hard off. Like you wanted.”

[2e0m8h4j] Cage leaned back and looked at Flynn as if seeing his outline against a different life. “You know what that means,” he said.

[7y1d5m8q] “That I can’t call anyone,” Flynn said.

[9n0c6v3t] “That you can’t run home and tell them you saw the monsters,” Cage corrected gently. “It means you’re here. Really here.”

[0x4k8p2m] Someone laughed softly from the other side of the fire. A thin man with a beard that came in patchy and tired. He wore grease under his nails like permanent jewelry. Wrench, Flynn guessed.

[k9m2t6w0] The person next to Wrench was younger, almost Flynn’s age, with bright eyes and restless hands that couldn’t stop moving. Birdie. He leaned forward like the story had already started and he didn’t want to miss the first word.

[3p8s1h5v] Diesel stayed standing. She didn’t sit. She watched the perimeter even while she listened, as if the desert might try to sneak in.

[1v6q0c2z] “Sit,” Cage said, and it wasn’t a request. He pointed at an upturned crate.

[6c5x9m1u] Flynn sat. The crate wobbled. He held himself still and tried not to look like someone pretending to belong.

[8m0p2q7d] Cage held a mug in both hands. The mug was chipped, the enamel worn through to metal at the rim. “You know why we do it?” he asked.

[4h1r8n6s] Flynn could have lied and said *freedom.* He could have said *cars.* The words in his throat were smaller. “Because… it’s real,” he said.

[n7q3m0b8] Cage’s smile widened just enough to show satisfaction. “Good. Because everything else is a screen.”

[5x0d9k2h] Birdie made a small sound, like agreement that needed no language.

[2n8p5v0c] Cage nodded toward the fire. “My father drove a ’69 Charger,” he said, and the way his voice changed on the word *father* made it feel like a tool he didn’t use often. “He drove it fast enough to make the world shut up.”

[s1r4k7m9] Diesel snorted. “Your father drove it to the liquor store,” she said.

[0m6h1q8y] Cage looked at her, amused. “And back. But he did it loud.”

[7k9m3t1x] Wrench’s laugh was quieter, like it had to ask permission to exist.

[3b0n7v5p] Cage kept going, not because he needed their approval but because the fire asked for stories. “My grandfather drove before the warnings. Before the speed governors. Before the roads got painted with sensors like veins.”

[9p2t0x6a] Flynn leaned in despite himself. He had grown up with the ban like weather. He’d never heard anyone talk about before like it was a place you could return to.

[6s7m1d0k] “When I was a kid,” Cage said, “they still let you own a real engine if you paid enough taxes and promised you’d only use it on ‘designated recreation routes.’ Like joy needed a permit.”

[1k8v2m9c] Birdie’s eyes shone. “My abuela said the cops used to pull you over for *driving too slow,*” he said, reverent, like myth.

[w0q5n1s7] Diesel shook her head. “Your abuela also says she saw a lion in the canal,” she said.

[8t1x6p3m] “Could’ve been,” Birdie insisted. “Desert’s got everything if you look hard enough.”

[2m6k9y0v] Cage watched the exchange like a priest watching children argue over scripture. “The point is,” he said, “they took it. They took the engines first because they said it was for the planet. Then they took the steering wheels because they said it was for our safety.”

[5v1n7c2d] Flynn felt his throat tighten. His implant’s absence made room for a different kind of pressure: the memory of the city’s soft voices and gentle chimes, always reminding you to comply.

[0y8p2h6k] “They came with trucks,” Cage said. “They came with cameras and a smile. They offered buybacks and credits and tax forgiveness. Like they were doing you a favor.”

[r9m0k3x1] Wrench stared into the fire, and for a second Flynn thought he saw something there—grief, maybe, or anger that had been worn down into ash.

[6h2q1v8s] “My dad hid his carburetor in the wall,” Wrench said quietly. “Like it was a gun.”

[4c7m0d3p] Birdie laughed, then stopped when he realized no one else was laughing.

[1n5s9q2x] Diesel finally sat, but only on the edge of a crate, knees ready to spring. “They don’t take them now,” she said. “Now they just make it impossible to keep them running. Fuel’s a felony. Parts are a felony. Sound is a felony.”

[9d3k8m0r] Cage nodded. “And they tell you you’re lucky,” he said. “Because you can’t crash. Because you can’t die. Because you can’t make a mistake big enough to matter.”

[7m1x4p6h] Flynn looked at his hands, at the faint oil stain he’d gotten from his own car’s dipstick last week. It was nothing compared to Diesel’s hands, compared to the permanent black under Wrench’s nails. He suddenly wanted to be dirtier.

[2p0c7v8n] “You from the city,” Cage said.

[0k6m1x9t] Flynn nodded.

[w3p9d2m0] “And you came out here anyway,” Cage said. “So what are you running from?”

[8c1n6h4q] Flynn hesitated. The easy answer was *nothing.* The true answer sat heavier. “The feeling that I’m already dead,” he said.

[5h7m2p0x] Birdie made a soft whistle like appreciation.

[1d0k8v6m] Diesel’s eyes narrowed, not unkindly. Like she was checking whether Flynn was serious enough to hurt.

[9x3m1n7c] Cage leaned forward. The fire reflected in his eyes like two small wrecks. “They revived me once,” he said, conversational, like bringing up an old injury. “Four minutes. That’s what it took.”

[6q2p0w8h] Flynn’s stomach dropped. He had seen the clip. Everyone had seen the clip—the outlaw who walked away from a clinic with blood on his shirt, refusing transport. It had been taken down within hours, but copies lived everywhere, passed like prayer.

[0s5n1c9k] “I didn’t ask for it,” Cage said. “Didn’t want it. But the system doesn’t care what you want. It cares what you cost.”

[3m7h0p2v] Wrench shifted, and Flynn saw the faint outline of old scars up his forearms—thin white ropes, as if he’d once been tied too tight.

[t2n9c1x4] “So we do this,” Cage said, gesturing with his mug toward the cars, toward the darkening desert. “We keep something alive they tried to bury. And when we drive, we get one honest minute where we’re not being guided.”

[8p1m5q0s] Flynn felt the words land in him like a hook. He had come for the machines, he realized. But he was staying for the permission.

[4v6n0h2m] Diesel stood again, unable to sit through the rest of Cage’s sermon. She walked to the man at the engine bay—the big one, the one who’d been throwing sparks—and tapped his shoulder.

[1h8q2m7d] The man straightened. He was taller than Cage, built like a door. His face was open, honest in a way that made him look out of place among mythmakers. He wiped his hands on a rag that had long since stopped being clean.

[9m3d7k0p] “Diesel,” he said, voice like gravel.

[0x9k4p1n] “Flynn,” Diesel said, jerking her chin. “Diesel’s Flynn. Flynn’s Diesel. This is Don.”

[6b1n8m3q] Don nodded once. His eyes lingered on Flynn’s clean shirt, the unscarred look of him. “You know how to turn a wrench?”

[2k7m0p5v] Flynn felt heat rise in his face. “I… can learn.”

[7p2n6x0m] Don’s mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. “Everyone can,” he said, and went back to the engine like it was a friend he’d promised not to abandon.

[1x0m8v3c] The sky bruised purple. The fire popped. Somewhere a coyote yipped and the sound made Flynn’s spine tighten with something like joy.

[4n6k2p9s] Cage watched Flynn watch the darkness. “You afraid?” he asked.

[9c3m7h0k] Flynn surprised himself by shaking his head. “No,” he said. “Not the way I was.”

[0h8p1m6q] Cage nodded, pleased. “Good. Fear’s useful. Panic’s not.”

[6m2p0x7d] Diesel came back carrying a small metal box the size of a lunch pail. She set it on the ground beside Flynn with a deliberate clunk. “Phone,” she said.

[2v7m1n9c] Flynn stared.

[8x0k5p2m] “Put it in,” Diesel said. “Faraday. No signals in or out. If you’re staying, you’re staying.”

[1p9m3d7k] Flynn pulled his phone from his pocket. The screen lit automatically, searching for a network, asking for permission to update, to sync, to keep him safe. He wanted to smash it.

[5k2n8m0v] He dropped it into the box. Diesel closed the lid and latched it.

[0n6p1x8q] The small click felt louder than it should have.

[r3m0k7v2] Cage leaned in, voice lower now, the show for the crew slipping away. “You can sleep in the trailer by the tools,” he said. “If you work. If you listen. If you don’t bring eyes down on us.”

[7h9m2p0x] Flynn nodded. “I won’t,” he said.

[1c8m0v5q] Cage held his gaze for a long moment, like he was weighing Flynn’s soul on a thumb. Then he smiled again, sharp and bright. “Tomorrow,” he said, “you get grease under those nails. We’ll see if you still want to belong.”

[9v1n6k0m] Around the fire, Birdie let out a breath he’d been holding. Wrench stared into the coals like he was watching a future burn.

[3x0p8m2d] Diesel picked up her rifle and made a slow circle of the Compound’s edge, eyes on the dark. Don’s tools rang softly as he kept working by generator light.

[6k2m7h0p] Flynn sat there until the stars came out, and with them a feeling he hadn’t had in years: the sense that the night was allowed to be dangerous, and that danger was not a malfunction.

[0m9p1x6s] When he finally stood, the crate leaving lines on his palms, Cage’s voice followed him like smoke. “Welcome,” Cage said, and it sounded almost like a curse.
